suggestion: The topic is strong enough to proceed. To push novelty toward 8-9, sharpen the contribution around the memory-allocation-as-strategy formalism rather than the game variant itself. Specifically: (1) Frame the primary contribution as a general framework for 'strategic forgetting' in imperfect-recall extensive-form games, with Blind Omok as the testbed — this elevates it from 'we built an AI for a TV show game' to 'we formalize and solve a new class of decision problems.' (2) The strongest publishable result would be a theoretical characterization of the memory-performance frontier (information-theoretic lower bounds on memory needed for epsilon-optimal play) paired with empirical demonstration that learned agents approach this bound. (3) Consider connecting to the absentminded driver problem (Piccione & Rubinstein 1997) and recent work on resource-rational agents (Lieder & Griffiths 2020) to ground the contribution in established theory. The feasibility score reflects that the asymmetric 1-player-vs-NPC formulation is tractable within the stated compute budget, but RL convergence on the full 19x19 board is uncertain — plan ablations on 9x9 and 15x15 as primary results with 19x19 as validation.
